annotations.label: Perturbation theory

anchor.snippet: perturbation theory is a set of approximation schemes directly related to mathematical perturbation

annotations.note: Quantum-mechanical perturbation theory is a physics framework with no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exactly solvable Hamiltonians

anchor.snippet: such as the hydrogen atom , the quantum harmonic oscillator and the particle in a box

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize quantum Hamiltonians like the hydrogen atom, harmonic oscillator, or particle in a box.

annotations.label: Applicability of perturbation theory

anchor.snippet: Perturbation theory is applicable if the problem at hand cannot be solved exactly

annotations.note: This applicability criterion is a physics heuristic absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stark effect

anchor.snippet: by adding a perturbative electric potential to the quantum mechanical model of the hydrogen atom

annotations.note: The Stark effect is a physics phenomenon not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Asymptotic series of perturbation expansions

anchor.snippet: the results are expressed in terms of finite power series

annotations.note: The claim about perturbation results being asymptotic series is a physics statement not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: QED electron magnetic moment

anchor.snippet: the calculation of the electron's magnetic moment has been found to agree with experiment to eleven decimal places

annotations.note: QED computation of the electron magnetic moment is an experimental physics result outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Invalidity for large perturbations

anchor.snippet: Under some circumstances, perturbation theory is an invalid approach to take

annotations.note: This caveat about large perturbations is a physics observation with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Failure for non-adiabatic states

anchor.snippet: Perturbation theory also fails to describe states that are not generated adiabatically

annotations.note: The adiabatic-failure statement is a physics claim absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cooper pairs in superconductivity

anchor.snippet: An example of this phenomenon may be found in conventional superconductivity

annotations.note: Cooper pairs and superconductivity are physics topics not modeled in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Time-independent perturbation theory

anchor.snippet: Time-independent perturbation theory is one of two categories of perturbation theory

annotations.note: Time-independent (Rayleigh–Schrödinger) perturbation theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unperturbed Hamiltonian

anchor.snippet: The process begins with an unperturbed Hamiltonian H 0 , which is assumed to have no time dependence

annotations.note: The notion of an unperturbed quantum Hamiltonian has no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perturbed Hamiltonian

anchor.snippet: Let V be a Hamiltonian representing a weak physical disturbance

annotations.note: The perturbing potential V as a quantum Hamiltonian is a physics object not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Power series expansion in λ

anchor.snippet: they can be written as a (Maclaurin) power series in λ

annotations.note: Mathlib has general formal/analytic power-series infrastructure (`HasFPowerSeriesAt`, `FormalMultilinearSeries`), but the specific perturbative ansatz for eigenstates/eigenvalues in λ is not formalized.

annotations.label: Zeroth-order equation

anchor.snippet: The zeroth-order equation is simply the Schrödinger equation for the unperturbed system

annotations.note: The Schrödinger equation and its order-by-order expansion are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First-order equation

anchor.snippet: The first-order equation is

annotations.note: The first-order perturbation equation is a physics derivation absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: First-order energy shift

anchor.snippet: This leads to the first-order energy shift

annotations.note: The first-order energy shift formula is a quantum-mechanics result not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First-order correction to eigenstate

anchor.snippet: The first-order change in the n -th energy eigenstate has a contribution from each of the other energy eigenstates

annotations.note: The first-order eigenstate correction formula is a physics result not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Validity condition (small matrix elements)

anchor.snippet: the perturbation theory can be legitimately used only when the absolute magnitude of the matrix elements of the perturbation is small

annotations.note: This validity criterion on matrix elements is a physics condition absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Second-order corrections

anchor.snippet: Up to second order, the expressions for the energies and (normalized) eigenstates are

annotations.note: Second-order energy and eigenstate correction formulas are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Third-order energy correction

anchor.snippet: the third-order energy correction can be shown to be

annotations.note: The third-order energy correction formula is a physics result absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fifth-order energy and fourth-order state corrections

anchor.snippet: then the energy corrections to fifth order can be written

annotations.note: High-order perturbative correction formulas are physics results not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Energy correction via correlation functions

anchor.snippet: It is possible to relate the k -th order correction to the energy E n to the k -point connected correlation function

annotations.note: Connected correlation functions and their relation to energy corrections are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Degenerate perturbation theory

anchor.snippet: Suppose that two or more energy eigenstates of the unperturbed Hamiltonian are degenerate

annotations.note: Degenerate perturbation theory is a physics framework with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: First-order degenerate perturbation

anchor.snippet: we need solve the perturbed Hamiltonian restricted to the degenerate subspace D

annotations.note: Diagonalizing the perturbation on the degenerate subspace is a physics procedure absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Higher-order degenerate corrections

anchor.snippet: Higher-order corrections due to other eigenstates outside D can be found in the same way as for the non-degenerate case

annotations.note: Higher-order degenerate corrections are physics results not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nearly free electron model

anchor.snippet: An application is found in the nearly free electron model

annotations.note: The nearly free electron model is a solid-state physics example not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Degeneracy lifted to first order setup

anchor.snippet: a perturbation that completely lifts the degeneracy to first order of correction

annotations.note: The lifted-degeneracy setup is a physics construction absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Energy and state corrections (lifted degeneracy)

anchor.snippet: we have the following formulae for the energy correction to the second order

annotations.note: Second-order corrections with lifted degeneracy are physics formulas not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multi-parameter generalization

anchor.snippet: The generalization of time-independent perturbation theory to the case where there are multiple small parameters

annotations.note: Multi-parameter perturbation theory is a physics generalization with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Parameterized Hamiltonian

anchor.snippet: a parameterized Hamiltonian is considered as a function defined on the parameter manifold

annotations.note: A Hamiltonian as a function on a parameter manifold is a physics construction not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linearly parameterized Hamiltonian

anchor.snippet: The following linearly parameterized Hamiltonian is frequently used

annotations.note: The linearly parameterized Hamiltonian is a physics object absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalized force operators

anchor.snippet: then F μ should be identified as the generalized force operators related to those coordinates

annotations.note: Generalized force operators are physics objects with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Adiabatic assumption / power series

anchor.section: Perturbation theory as power series expansion
anchor.snippet: The validity of perturbation theory lies on the adiabatic assumption

annotations.note: The adiabatic assumption underlying perturbative power series is a physics premise absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: First Hellmann–Feynman theorem

anchor.snippet: The first Hellmann–Feynman theorem gives the derivative of the energy

annotations.note: The Hellmann–Feynman theorem is not formalized in Mathlib (the only 'Feynman' reference is an unrelated comment about path integrals).

annotations.label: Second Hellmann–Feynman theorem

anchor.snippet: The second Hellmann–Feynman theorem gives the derivative of the state

annotations.note: The second Hellmann–Feynman theorem has no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Second-order energy correction (geometric)

anchor.snippet: To the second order, the energy correction reads

annotations.note: The geometric second-order energy correction is a physics result absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Second-order state correction (geometric)

anchor.snippet: The same computational scheme is applicable for the correction of states

annotations.note: The geometric second-order state correction is a physics result not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Effective Hamiltonian

anchor.snippet: the effective Hamiltonian in the low energy subspace reads

annotations.note: The effective low-energy Hamiltonian is a physics construction with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Time-dependent perturbation theory

anchor.snippet: studies the effect of a time-dependent perturbation V ( t ) applied to a time-independent Hamiltonian

annotations.note: Time-dependent perturbation theory is a physics framework not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Occupation probability

anchor.snippet: The square of the absolute amplitude c n ( t ) is the probability that the system is in state n at time t

annotations.note: The Born-rule occupation probability is a physics statement absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Coupled equations for amplitudes

anchor.snippet: this can be reduced to a set of coupled differential equations for the amplitudes

annotations.note: The coupled amplitude equations from variation of constants are physics derivations not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Iterative (perturbative) solution

anchor.snippet: Repeatedly substituting this expression for c n back into right hand side, yields an iterative solution

annotations.note: The iterative perturbative solution for amplitudes is a physics procedure absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fermi's golden rule

anchor.snippet: such as Fermi's golden rule , which relates the rate of transitions between quantum states to the density of states

annotations.note: Fermi's golden rule is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dyson series

anchor.snippet: where T is the time ordering operator

annotations.note: The time-ordered Dyson series is a physics construction with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: First-order transition amplitude

anchor.snippet: The corresponding transition probability amplitude to first order is

annotations.note: The first-order transition amplitude is a physics result absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Time-independent PT within Dyson series

anchor.snippet: time-independent perturbation theory is also organized inside this time-dependent perturbation theory Dyson series

annotations.note: Embedding time-independent PT within the Dyson series is a physics observation not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dual Dyson series

anchor.snippet: a dual Dyson series , is meaningful at large

annotations.note: The dual Dyson series for strong coupling is a physics construction absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Duality principle

anchor.snippet: This is called duality principle in perturbation theory

annotations.note: The duality principle in perturbation theory is a physics concept with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Wigner-Kirkwood series

anchor.snippet: The Wigner-Kirkwood series is a semiclassical series with eigenvalues given exactly as for WKB approximation

annotations.note: The Wigner–Kirkwood semiclassical series and WKB approximation are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchor.section: Example of first-order perturbation theory – ground-state energy of the quartic oscillator
anchor.snippet: Consider the quantum harmonic oscillator with the quartic potential perturbation

annotations.note: The quartic-oscillator ground-state energy example is a physics computation absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum pendulum

anchor.section: Example of first- and second-order perturbation theory – quantum pendulum
anchor.snippet: Consider the quantum-mathematical pendulum with the Hamiltonian

annotations.note: The quantum pendulum example is a physics computation not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Free particle with weak potential

anchor.snippet: When the unperturbed state is a free motion of a particle with kinetic energy

annotations.note: The free-particle-with-weak-potential example is a physics computation absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hilbert space

anchor.snippet: an Hermitian operator H ( x μ ) that acts on the Hilbert space

annotations.note: Hilbert spaces are formalized in Mathlib as `InnerProductSpace` (combined with `CompleteSpace`).

annotations.label: Hermitian operator

anchor.snippet: Thus, V is formally a Hermitian operator

annotations.note: Hermitian/symmetric linear operators on inner-product spaces are formalized as `LinearMap.IsSymmetric` (with `IsSelfAdjoint` also available for the bounded/star setting).

annotations.label: Maclaurin / Taylor series

anchor.section: Perturbation theory as power series expansion
anchor.snippet: such that their values in the vicinity region can be calculated in power series (like Taylor expansion ) of the parameters

annotations.note: Taylor/Maclaurin series and analytic-function power series are formalized via `HasFPowerSeriesAt` and `FormalMultilinearSeries`.

annotations.label: Vector bundle over parameter manifold

anchor.snippet: the states [MATH] form a vector bundle over the parameter manifold

annotations.note: Vector bundles are formalized in Mathlib as the `VectorBundle` typeclass (with smooth variants in `Mathlib.Geometry.Manifold.VectorBundle`).

annotations.label: Covariant derivative / connection

anchor.section: Perturbation theory as power series expansion
anchor.snippet: it should be understood as the covariant derivative if the vector bundle is equipped with non-vanishing connection

annotations.note: General connections/covariant derivatives on vector bundles are not yet formalized in Mathlib in this differential-geometric sense.

annotations.label: Lie derivative

anchor.snippet: In terms of Lie derivatives

annotations.note: Lie derivatives on smooth manifolds are not currently formalized in Mathlib at this level of generality.

annotations.label: Inverse Laplace transform

anchor.snippet: one has to consider the inverse Laplace transform

annotations.note: The Laplace transform and its inverse are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Orthonormal basis of eigenstates

anchor.snippet: Given that the eigenstates of the Hamiltonian always form an orthonormal basis

annotations.note: Orthonormal bases of Hilbert spaces are formalized in Mathlib as `HilbertBasis` (built on the `Orthonormal` predicate).

annotations.label: Resolution of the identity

anchor.snippet: By using the resolution of the identity

annotations.note: While orthonormal-basis completeness exists in Mathlib, the spectral 'resolution of the identity' for self-adjoint operators is not formalized.

annotations.label: Time-ordering operator

anchor.snippet: where T is the time ordering operator

annotations.note: The time-ordering operator is a physics construct with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.note: The interaction/Dirac picture transformation is a quantum-mechanics construction not in Mathlib.

annotations.note: The Schrödinger picture of quantum dynamics is a physics framework not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Bra–ket notation is a physics notational device; Mathlib uses inner-product notation instead.
